item.title: Planning for Tax Rises
item.description: Everybody wants to see tax rises, except the British economy. How can we do both?

item.title: UK Sectoral Balances
item.description: Sectoral Finacial Balances for the UK as a % of GDP, smoothed over a year

item.title: It's Just Debits and Credits
item.description: When using bookkeeping tools to do stock/flow analysis, it’s important to remember that we are just doing debits and credits and summing them up into stocks of debits and credits. We are using the tools of journals and balance sheets to do economic analysis.

item.title: The Perpetual Problem With Interest Rates
item.description: How can you be worried about interest rises when you’re issuing fixed interest rate securities? Isn’t that sort of the point of fixing the interest rate?

item.title: Anatomy of an FX Transaction
item.description: How exactly does an FX transaction works in a floating currency rate environment? Read on to find out

item.title: How Money is Created in the UK
item.description: Money is created in the UK by order of HM Treasury. Here’s how it works

item.title: It's the Exporters Stupid
item.description: A very good article here that comes closer than most to what I consider the crunch issue. The key point is that if a currency moves down so that imports become ‘more expensive’, then the ‘inflation’ that goes off is a distributional response that tries to eliminate some of those imports so that the exchange demands equalise. That also eliminates somebody else’s exports. The important thing to remember is that when a currency goes down, all the others in the world go up in relation to it and nations that rely upon exports (export led nations) start to lose trade - which depresses their own economy.

item.title: Resources Are People Too
item.description: The Resources you are looking for are in the Unemployment Queue

item.title: Why Banks Pay Interest on Deposits
item.description: How the central bank setting interest rates above zero forces banks to pay interest on deposits

item.description: Another neoliberal throws in the towel in the face of unassailable facts and logic

item.description: Why there is no need to raise taxes just because more government bonds have been issued

item.description: Overview of a UK Job Guarantee

item.title: Another Fixed Rate Inconsistency?
item.description: Why do those people who agitate for a fixed exchange rate between currencies also advocate for a floating rate between government bonds and the currency? If a move between bonds and currency needs to be discouraged by a floating rate, then why doesn’t a move between a local currency and a foreign currency need to be discouraged by a floating rate? If the Euro is such a great idea across such a wide area, why aren’t Eurozone government bonds payable at par on demand at any bank in the Eurozone?

item.title: Robin Hood: MMT Edition
item.description: Updated Robin Hood Script for an Modern Money Theory World

item.title: Running a Modern Money Economy
item.description: Putting it all together

item.title: Savings Are an Export Product
item.description: Things make a lot more sense when you realise other currency areas love your savings products — a lot

item.title: Labour Hours and Labour Services
item.description: Employment is about buying the former and generating the latter. How does that relate to the Job Guarantee?

item.title: Basic Income: Thief of Time
item.description: Basic income is a desire to be paid twice - once self consuming your own hours and once consuming the output of somebody else’s hours

item.title: Job Guarantee: Jobs for the People
item.description: How the Job Guarantee solves the matching problem

item.title: This is how You Abolish Tuition Fees
item.description: Student debt is another set of debt that isn’t really a debt. So just cancel it all
